title: Addiction and Obsessive Compulsive Disorder
subtitle: Why is co-occurring OCD and addiction so common?
author: Caroline

seo.title: Addiction and Obsessive Compulsive Disorder | Plan Your Recovery
seo.description: Addiction and Obsessive Compulsive Disorder. Evidence-based information on addiction and mental health from Plan Your Recovery, St. Louis.

body:
OCD sometimes manifests through someone feeling compelled to count, or place items into a specific order.

What is OCD?

OCD is marked by either the presence of unwanted obsessions, unwanted compulsive behaviors, or both.

Obsessions

Obsessions are repeated intrusive thoughts and mental images that case distress and are unwanted. Many obsessions invoke fear -- for example, a person may experience obsessive thoughts about driving their car off the side of the road even when they have no desire to do so. Other examples include obsessive fears of:

  • Contracting a deadly illness
  • Doing something that goes against one’s religious faith
  • A partner leaving them or accidentally cheating on their partner
  • Doing something that magically leads to a terrible outcome.

It is important to note that all humans experience intrusive thoughts. Many people have, for example, a passing thought of what would happen if they drove their car off the side of the road, or if they said something terrible to a friend. The difference is that the person without OCD is more likely to perceive the thought as what it is: mental noise that they will not act on.

People with OCD often feel that experiencing the thought is shameful. They sometimes feel that its simple occurrence is as bad as acting out the thought.

Persons with OCD also actively attempt to repress their unwanted intrusive thoughts. Unfortunately, this generally causes the thought to occur more often and with greater attached shame.

Compulsions

Most often, the compulsions of OCD are used by the individual in an effort to help them exert control over fearful obsessions. These compulsions are usually ritualistic and repetitive. They may involve counting, checking, ordering, or cleaning.

Compulsive hand washing is a common symptom in individuals who experience contamination obsessions.

Sometimes, compulsions seem clearly connected to obsessions. Examples include:

  • A person who is obsessed with germs cleaning the sink 15 times.
  • A person who is afraid of contracting a deadly illness calling their doctor several times a week.
  • A person who has intrusive thoughts that go against her religious faith spending hours each day praying for forgiveness.

Other compulsions are less obvious to outside viewers. They make sense only to the person with OCD. Examples include:

  • A person who compulsively counts to ten every time they see a bird.
  • A person who reties his shoes until they are tied "perfectly".
  • A person who says "pineapple, banana, beach" after they do something that they fear is offensive.

OCD is exhausting and can feel overwhelming.

Regardless of the apparent logic of the compulsions, they provide some relief to the individual with OCD.

However, this relief is extremely temporary. A person may experience a lapse in anxiety for a few seconds after engaging in a compulsion. That anxiety will quickly return, along with intrusive thoughts of the obsession. Thus, the individual repeats the compulsion over and over and over, in search of permanent relief that never comes.

How do OCD and substance use overlap?

OCD is exhausting and time consuming. Some individuals with OCD engage in substance use in an effort to numb themselves to their obsessions. This sense of being numbed also temporarily frees them from their compulsions.

Though these substances may provide temporary relief, heavy use leads to worse functioning in major domains of life. Ultimately, substance use works no better than the original compulsive behavior. Both are time-consuming and intrusive and do not lead to long-term improvement. A person may spend even more time and energy buying, consuming, and recovering from their substance of choice than they originally spent in performing compulsions.

Substances are often used by people with OCD to mute their obsessions.

Other people with co-occurring addiction and OCD have obsessions about their substance or addictive behavior of choice. Thus, disengaging from that substance or behavior becomes even more challenging.

How is co-occurring OCD and addiction treated?

Exposure therapy is the standard treatment for obsessive compulsive disorder. However, most CBT therapists agree that exposure therapy cannot take place when a substance use disorder is active. Substance use can lessen the helpfulness of the exposure, lowering the amount that the person learns during therapy.

Thus, a person cannot be actively using substances when they begin OCD treatment. Once they are in early recovery, the root cause of their substance use, OCD, can be effectively targeted by a therapist.
